title: Time-resolved XRISM spectroscopy reveals the evolution and structure of the corona in MCG-6-30-15
comment: 28 pages, 8 figures. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We present a time-resolved analysis of high-resolution spectra of the AGN MCG-6-30-15 obtained by XRISM alongside broadband spectra from NuSTAR and XMM-Newton during a coordinated observing campaign in February 2024. These observations provide some of the most detailed measurements of X-ray reflection from the innermost regions of the accretion disc around a supermassive black hole, and its evolution during periods of significant variability. We find that both the X-ray spectrum and its variability can be described by a self-consistent model of the reflection of the coronal X-ray emission from the accretion disc around a rapidly-spinning (a > 0.93) black hole, in which the observed variability arises from underlying changes in the luminosity, spatial extent and motion of the corona. While the corona is compact, residing within 10rg of the black hole for the majority of the observations, finite spatial extent is required to fully explain the shape of the reflection spectrum. A flare was observed in the X-ray emission during which the corona expanded to around 15rg and was accelerated away from the black hole reaching a velocity of 0.27c. Around the flare were short dips in the observed flux, during which the corona was found to have collapsed to a confined region, within 2.5rg of the black hole, enhancing the relativistic effects observed from the inner accretion disc. We find it is necessary to account for such significant spectral variation in order to obtain accurate measurements of the spin of the black hole via X-ray reflection spectroscopy.

AI.tldr: The study uses XRISM, NuSTAR, and XMM-Newton data to analyze X-ray reflection from MCG-6-30-15's accretion disc, revealing corona dynamics affecting black hole spin measurements.
AI.motivation: To understand how variable corona properties impact X-ray reflection spectra and black hole spin determinations in AGN.
AI.method: Time-resolved spectral analysis of high-resolution X-ray data from coordinated observations, employing a self-consistent reflection model accounting for corona luminosity, size, and motion changes.
AI.result: Observed corona expansion/contraction (2.5-15 rg), velocity changes (up to 0.27c), and relativistic effects during flares/dips, showing dynamic corona behavior necessitates spectral variation considerations for accurate spin measurements.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic corona behavior significantly influences X-ray reflection signatures; neglecting temporal variations leads to inaccurate black hole spin estimates, emphasizing the need for time-resolved studies in AGN research.

title: High-energy neutrino constraints on primordial black holes as dark matter
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, Supplemental Material (6 pages, 6 figures)
summary: Primordial black holes (PBHs) are one of the most appealing dark matter candidates over a wide range of masses and abundances. This broad parameter space has been constrained by a variety of observational probes. In this work, for the first time, we use data from high-energy neutrino telescopes, like IceCube and ANTARES, to constrain sub-asteroid mass ($\lesssim 10^{18}\,\mathrm{g}$) Schwarzschild PBHs with extended mass functions. We derive limits from the diffuse high-energy neutrino flux produced by the direct evaporation of PBHs, as well as from the transient signatures associated with PBHs passing in the vicinity of the Earth. While our bounds are slightly weaker than existing constraints from gamma-ray observations, they provide an independent and complementary probe based on observational high-energy neutrino data. We further show that future detectors such as IceCube-Gen2 and KM3NeT can significantly improve these constraints, potentially excluding PBHs with masses up to $\sim \mathrm{few} \times 10^{18}\,\mathrm{g}$ composing the entirety of dark matter.

AI.tldr: This paper uses high-energy neutrino telescope data from IceCube and ANTARES to constrain sub-asteroid mass primordial black holes (PBHs) as dark matter candidates, offering complementary constraints to existing gamma-ray limits and suggesting future detectors could significantly improve these bounds.
AI.motivation: To explore PBHs as dark matter by leveraging neutrino data, providing an independent observational method and addressing gaps in existing constraints for low-mass PBHs ($\lesssim 10^{18}\,\mathrm{g}$).
AI.method: Analyzed neutrino flux from PBH evaporation and transient signals when PBHs pass near Earth. Used IceCube and ANTARES data to set constraints, comparing with gamma-ray results.
AI.result: Current neutrino-based limits are slightly weaker than gamma-ray constraints but offer complementary data. Future detectors like IceCube-Gen2 and KM3NeT could exclude PBHs up to a few 10^{18} g as primary dark matter constituents.
AI.conclusion: Neutrino telescopes provide a valuable independent probe for PBH dark matter. Upcoming experiments may significantly strengthen constraints, potentially confirming or ruling out PBHs in this mass range as dark matter components.

title: Repopulating the pair-instability mass gap without sustained growth to massive IMBHs: the case of 47\,Tuc
summary: We model the formation and retention of the most massive black hole (BH) in 47~Tuc using the semi-analytical code \texttt{cBHBd}, coupling cluster evolution with binary BH dynamics and computing merger-remnant masses, spins, and gravitational-wave recoil kicks via numerical-relativity surrogate prescriptions. We evolve 80\,000 cluster realisations spanning initial masses, densities, IMFs, and metallicities, in both a baseline scenario ($m_{\rm max} = 130\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$) and an extended-IMF scenario with ${\sim}\,50-110$ primordial BH seeds above the pair-instability gap ($M_{\rm BH} \sim 130-700\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$). Selecting models reproducing 47~Tuc's present-day mass and half-mass radius, we find hierarchical mergers alone yield a most massive retained BH of $M_{\rm BH} \sim 45-70\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$ with spin $χ_{\rm BH} \sim 0.65$, limited to ${\sim}\,1-3$ mergers, as second-generation remnants acquire spin $χ\sim 0.7$ that amplifies recoil kicks in subsequent generations. When primordial seeds are included, the retained-mass distribution becomes bimodal -- in ${\sim}\,90\%$ of realisations all seeds are ejected, but in ${\sim}\,10\%$ a massive seed ($M_{\rm BH} \gtrsim 450\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$) survives -- while the joint mass-spin distribution is trimodal; seeds surviving via stellar-mass BH mergers retain low spin ($χ\lesssim 0.3$), whereas seed-seed mergers produce high-mass, high-spin remnants ($χ\sim 0.65-0.7$), yielding 90th-percentile retained masses of ${\sim}\,500-1100\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$. Both scenarios are consistent with the $3σ$ dynamical upper limit of $578\,\mathrm{M}_{\odot}$. Our results favour a dark-remnant subsystem over a single massive IMBH and provide a spin-mass diagnostic testable with LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA, the Einstein Telescope, Cosmic Explorer, and LISA.

AI.tldr: The study uses simulations to model the formation of the most massive black hole in 47 Tucanae, considering both hierarchical mergers and primordial black hole seeds. The results show a bimodal distribution in retained black hole masses and suggest that observations from gravitational wave detectors can test the model.
AI.motivation: To understand the current mass and structure of 47 Tuc's black holes, explaining discrepancies without an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) while aligning with observational limits.
AI.method: Simulated 80,000 cluster realizations with semi-analytical code cBHBd, varying initial parameters. Tested two scenarios: hierarchical mergers alone or including primordial BH seeds beyond the pair-instability gap.
AI.result: Without primordial seeds, retained BH mass is 45-70 M⊙ with moderate spins (0.65). With primordial seeds, 10% cases retain massive BHs (450-1100 M⊙), creating a trimodal mass-spin distribution. Recoil kicks dominate ejection dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The data favors multiple smaller dark remnants over a single IMBH. Future gravitational wave detectors can distinguish scenarios via BH spin-mass correlations.

title: JWST Nebular Spectroscopy of SN 2023qov: Circumstellar Dust Emission in a Normal Type Ia Supernova
summary: We present panchromatic observations of the Type Ia supernova (SN Ia) 2023qov, ranging from $\sim$2 weeks before to $\sim$1 year after maximum light. \textit{JWST} near- and mid-infrared spectra at $+$276 and $+$363~days show $\sim$400 K dust emission that cools by $\sim$75 K between epochs, the first unambiguous spectroscopic detection of dust emission in a normal SN Ia. We find that the emission is well described by models of carbonaceous dust placed within $\sim$1 light year of the SN, with a dust mass of $\sim$$10^{-4}$ M$_{\odot}$. We do not see evidence of active dust creation, suggesting an infrared light echo by pre-existing circumstellar dust as the likely source of the emission. The \textit{JWST} nebular line profiles suggest asymmetric, stratified ejecta, similar to other normal SNe Ia, though a slight double-horn structure in the argon lines indicate a toroidal enhancement. SN 2023qov exhibits a slightly red, fast-declining early light curve ($Δm_{15}(B) = 1.47 \pm 0.05$ mag), from which we determine a $^{56}$Ni mass of $M_{56} = 0.21 \pm 0.04$ M$_{\odot}$, and a distance of $d = 36.0 \pm 1.8$ Mpc to the SN and its host, NGC 7029.

AI.tldr: Panchromatic observations of SN Ia 2023qov reveal dust emission via JWST spectra, suggesting a light echo from pre-existing circumstellar dust and asymmetric ejecta with toroidal argon enhancements. The SN's fast-declining light curve indicates 0.21 M_sun Ni-56 and a 36 Mpc distance to NGC 7029.
AI.motivation: To study dust properties, ejecta structure, and progenitor environment of a normal Type Ia supernova using JWST's unprecedented infrared capabilities, and to link observational features to explosion models.
AI.method: Multi-wavelength observations from pre-maximum to ~1 year post-maximum, including JWST's NIR and MIR spectra to detect dust emission. Analyzed nebular line profiles for ejecta asymmetry and derived physical parameters via light curve modeling.
AI.result: First unambiguous detection of ~400 K dust emission in a normal SN Ia, cooling by ~75 K over 87 days. Dust mass ~1e-4 M_sun located ~1 light-year from SN. Inferred pre-existing circumstellar dust light echo. Ejecta shows stratification with toroidal argon enhancement. SN has 0.21 M_sun Ni-56 and NGC 7029 is 36 Mpc distant.
AI.conclusion: Findings support pre-existing dust in SN Ia environments rather than in-situ creation, provide constraints on progenitor systems, and demonstrate JWST's role in studying SN circumstellar interaction and ejecta structure.

title: Visible Neutrino Decay As An Open Quantum System
summary: Decays of heavier neutrino mass eigenstates into lighter ones, while very slow in the Standard Model, can be significantly enhanced in scenarios with more than three neutrino flavours, or in models with new ultra-light particles such as Majorons. A full theoretical description is challenging due to the intricate interplay between oscillations and decay, interference between different decay channels, and the possibility of multi-step decay cascades. In this paper, we develop a fully general description of arbitrarily complex systems of oscillating and decaying neutrinos using methods from the theory of open quantum systems. Notably, we demonstrate how such systems can be implemented using the Lindblad master equation, the Liouvillian superoperator, as well as Kraus operators. The last two methods eschew the need for solving a differential equation, thereby showing superior numerical performance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a comprehensive framework using open quantum system methods (Lindblad master equation, Liouvillian superoperator, Kraus operators) to model complex systems of oscillating and decaying neutrinos, offering numerical efficiency improvements over traditional approaches.
AI.motivation: To tackle challenges in describing neutrino decay-oscillation interplay, multi-step cascades, and interference effects in beyond-Standard-Model scenarios with extra neutrino flavors or ultra-light particles like Majorons.
AI.method: Developed a general theoretical description via open quantum system formalisms, specifically implementing Lindblad master equations, Liouvillian superoperators, and Kraus operators to capture decay-oscillation dynamics without requiring differential equation solutions.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Liouvillian and Kraus operator methods avoid differential equation solving, leading to better numerical performance for complex multi-step decay cascades.
AI.conclusion: Open quantum system methods provide a numerically efficient and versatile tool for analyzing neutrino systems with combined oscillation and decay dynamics, applicable to beyond-Standard-Model physics scenarios.

title: Exact Gravastar Solution
summary:
Astrophysical black holes arise as exact solutions of the Einstein field equations. Therefore, any alternative, such as a gravastar, must satisfy the same level of mathematical rigor and internal consistency. A physically viable gravastar model should not rely on approximations or ad hoc matching of regions, but instead provide a single, exact, and self-consistent solution of the Einstein field equations throughout the entire spacetime.
  In this work, we propose an exact solution to the Einstein field equations in the context of gravitational vacuum stars (gravastars), originally introduced by Mazur and Mottola. This framework presents an alternative end state of gravitational collapse, leading to the formation of a compact object distinct from a classical black hole.
  Our model is constructed by dividing the gravastar into three regions, each described by exact solutions of the Einstein field equations. We analyze the key physical properties of the resulting configuration and examine its theoretical consistency and astrophysical viability. This study provides a clear and systematic assessment of gravastars as potential alternatives to black holes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an exact solution for gravastars, challenging traditional black hole models by providing a mathematically rigorous alternative that meets Einstein's equations entirely.
AI.motivation: To address the need for viable alternatives to black holes that are equally mathematically rigorous, avoiding approximations or ad hoc matching methods.
AI.method: Dividing the gravastar into three regions with exact Einstein equation solutions and analyzing their physical properties and consistency.
AI.result: A self-consistent gravastar model meeting Einstein's equations throughout all spacetime regions is presented, strengthening gravastars as potential black hole alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Gravastars are validated as theoretically consistent and astrophysically viable alternatives to classical black holes through this exact solution framework.

title: Even a precessing clock is right twice per orbit -- The super-periods of eRO-QPE2 and challenges for quasi-periodic eruption orbital models
summary: We present O$-$C (``observed minus calculated'') timing analysis of the quasi-periodic eruption (QPE) source eRO-QPE2 with a multi-mission X-ray campaign, which includes 32 observed eruptions spanning a month (i.e. 325 QPE cycles). In relation to accretion (e.g. disk instability) models, the O-C is consistent with a damped random walk of the QPE recurrence, albeit with highly uncertain parameters. If instead an underlying orbital clock is present, eRO-QPE2 is consistent with a period of $P \sim 2.24$\,h and two hierarchical super-periodic modulations, with periods of $\sim 4.4$\,d ($\sim47$\,P) and $\approx 95$\,d ($\approx 1000$\,P). We found no negative period derivative, with $|\dot{P}| \lesssim 2 \times 10^{-6}$\,s/s at $3σ$. This disfavors high-eccentricity WDs and high-mass/eccentricity IMBHs via GW decay. For disk-collision models, where the $\dot{P}$ from gas drag and the QPE integrated energy provide bounds on the local disk density, a main-sequence star is disfavored as EMRI secondary unless stellar debris streams are present, while stripped stars remain allowed. The correlated odd/even O-C disfavors both disk crossings per orbit being observed. Interpreting the data with one \emph{observed} event per orbit, the short modulation is consistent with apsidal precession for $a \sim 140\,R_g$, $e \approx 0.1$, and $M_{\rm BH} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{5}\,M_\odot$. The longer modulation (much less constrained) is inconsistent with EMRI nodal precession and disk precession is allowed for a limited parameter volume, while there is a solution with a stable hierarchical triple system with an outer massive black hole at $\sim 0.4\,\mathrm{mpc}$ and mass $\sim(0.1-1) \times M_{\rm BH}$. However, no reliable solution can be found with more robust EMRI trajectory models, possibly due to narrow likelihood peaks in a multi-dimensional parameter space with sparse data.

AI.tldr: The analysis of eRO-QPE2's X-ray eruptions using O-C timing reveals a damped random walk in eruption recurrence and potential orbital and super-periodic modulations, disfavoring high-eccentricity white dwarfs and certain IMBH scenarios. Disk-collision models and hierarchical triple systems are considered, but robust EMRI models lack reliable solutions.
AI.motivation: To investigate the underlying mechanisms of quasi-periodic eruptions (QPEs) in eRO-QPE2, exploring accretion models like disk instability and orbital dynamics involving binary or hierarchical systems.
AI.method: Performed O-C timing analysis on 32 eruptions over a month using multi-mission X-ray data, testing accretion models (disk instability) and orbital models (binary with super-periodic modulations).
AI.result: Observed damped random walk in QPE recurrence; identified orbital period ~2.24h and two super-periods (~4.4 days and ~95 days). Excluded high-eccentricity WDs/IMBHs via GW decay. Disk models require specific densities to disfavor main-sequence stars as EMRI secondaries. Hierarchical triple systems are a potential solution for the longer modulation.
AI.conclusion: The data favor orbital modulations and hierarchical systems over accretion disk instability, but lack conclusive EMRI models due to parameter space complexity. Further observations needed to better constrain parameters and test hierarchical triple hypotheses.

title: Feynman integral reduction by covariant differentiation
summary: We show how a large class of Feynman integrals can be efficiently reduced to master integrals by suitable covariant differentiation on the vector space dual to the one spanned by the master integrals. The connections needed in the covariant derivatives have to be built only once for a given topology and then apply to any configuration of internal propagator masses. We implement our algorithm in the Mathematica code Method for Reduction of Loop Integrals (MERLIN).

AI.tldr: The paper presents a systematic method for reducing Feynman integrals to master integrals through covariant differentiation in dual vector spaces, implemented in the Mathematica code MERLIN.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and general approach for reducing Feynman integrals that works for any internal propagator mass configuration in a given topology.
AI.method: The method uses covariant differentiation on the dual vector space of master integrals, constructing connections once per topology that are mass configuration-independent.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm enables efficient reduction of a class of Feynman integrals, validated through implementation in MERLIN.
AI.conclusion: The covariant differentiation approach offers a powerful, topology-specific reduction technique that avoids re-computing connections for different mass configurations, simplifying loop integral computations.

title: Extended Gravity Theories from a Thermodynamic Perspective
summary: We extend the thermodynamic derivation of gravity in the Jacobson framework by generalizing the Clausius relation through a nontrivial entropy functional. We show that entropy deformations appear as modifications of the effective gravitational coupling, defining a broad class of modified gravity theories. However, conventional entropy corrections are insufficient to resolve spacetime singularities within this approach. We then propose a new entropy form by incorporating quantum properties at the level of horizon degrees of freedom. Implementing this entropy in the modified gravitational framework, we study its cosmological implications at both early and late times. In the early Universe, the model predicts a nonsingular phase with a finite Hubble parameter, leading to a de Sitter-like inflationary expansion with finite entropy and temperature. At late times, the theory reproduces, at leading order, the effective dynamics of loop quantum cosmology.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new entropy form incorporating quantum properties of horizon degrees of freedom to address spacetime singularities in modified gravity theories, leading to nonsingular early Universe scenarios and late-time dynamics resembling loop quantum cosmology.
AI.motivation: To extend Jacobson's thermodynamic gravity derivation by addressing the limitation of conventional entropy corrections in resolving spacetime singularities.
AI.method: Generalizing the Clausius relation with a nontrivial entropy functional and proposing a new entropy form based on quantum properties of horizon degrees of freedom. This entropy is incorporated into modified gravity theories to study cosmological implications.
AI.result: The model predicts a nonsingular early Universe with finite Hubble parameter and de Sitter-like inflation, and late-time dynamics matching loop quantum cosmology's leading-order behavior.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully modifies gravitational couplings via quantum-informed entropy to resolve singularities and align with observational cosmology across cosmic eras.

title: High Resolution X-ray Spectroscopy of the Nova-Like Cataclysmic Variable BZ Cam using Chandra HETG: Diagnosis of the ADAF-like (Advective) Hot Flow
comment: 21 pages, 6 Figures and 4 Tables, accepted to be published in the Astrophysical Journal as it stands
summary: Nova-likes such as BZ Cam are high state Cataclysmic Variables showing hard X-ray emission that can be characterized with advective hot flows in the inner accretion disk. We explore Chandra High Energy Transmission Grating (HETG) observations of BZ~Cam for detailed line diagnosis and ionization conditions in the X-ray regime. We mostly find H- and He-like emission lines of Mg, Si, S, and Fe. All He-like line components of forbidden, intercombination and resonance lines are present. The R ratios of selected lines indicate plasma densities of a few $\times$10$^{12-14}$ cm$^{-3}$ and G ratios reveal temperatures (3-6)$\times$ 10$^6$ K where the Fe lines yield (1-3)$\times$ 10$^7$ K. The H to He line ratios and the R and G ratios show that the plasma is in a nonequilibrium ionization condition, which is consistent with our previous X-ray results and the accretion flow in the X-ray region being an ADAF-like (advective) hot flow. Simultaneous fits of the HEG and MEG spectra or the broadband joint spectra of ROSAT, Chandra zero order and NuSTAR yield temperatures 3.4-6.3 keV using a VNEI model of plasma emission (in XSPEC) or Bremsstrahlung emission. An additional power law is detected above 98\% Confidence Level in the broadband analysis. The orbital variations and the broadband spectra show dipping/veiling of the X-rays and an additional warm absorber model with an ionization parameter log($ξ$) = 2.7 is required at the 3$σ$ level, along with the VNEI model where the HEG and MEG simultaneous fits yield the log($ξ$) = 3.6 .

AI.tldr: The study analyzes X-ray spectra of BZ Cam, a Nova-like Cataclysmic Variable, using Chandra HETG data. It identifies high-ionization emission lines from Mg, Si, S, and Fe, indicating plasma in a nonequilibrium ionization state consistent with an ADAF-like accretion flow. Broadband fitting reveals temperatures of 3.4-6.3 keV and detects a significant power law component, alongside a warm absorber.
AI.motivation: To investigate ionization conditions and plasma properties in the X-ray emitting region of BZ Cam, aiming to understand the nature of its advective hot flow and the role of nonequilibrium ionization processes.
AI.method: The team analyzed Chandra HETG spectra (HEG/MEG) and combined them with ROSAT, Chandra zero-order, and NuSTAR data. They measured emission line ratios (R, G) to derive plasma density (~1e12-1e14 cm⁻³) and temperature (3-6e6 K, up to 1e7 K for Fe). They used VNEI plasma models and Bremsstrahlung fits, incorporating warm absorber models for spectral analysis.
AI.result: Key findings include nonequilibrium ionization states, detection of a 98% confidence power law component, and a required warm absorber (log ξ = 2.7-3.6). Temperatures from broadband fits are 3.4-6.3 keV, supporting an ADAF-like accretion scenario with veiling effects during orbital variations.
AI.conclusion: The data confirm that BZ Cam's X-ray emission originates from an ADAF-like advective flow under nonequilibrium ionization. The presence of a warm absorber and power law component suggests complex geometry and emission processes, reinforcing the need for multiwavelength studies to fully characterize these systems.

title: A Consistent Treatment of Final-State Interactions in NuWro Quasielastic Channel
summary: In this proceeding, I present a modified treatment of final-state interactions (FSI) in quasielastic (QE) lepton-nucleus scattering within the spectra function (SF) framework of the NuWro Monte Carlo generator. Our approach establishes a consistent correspondence between inclusive cross-section calculations and exclusive descriptions of hadron-propagation by combining a convolution-based formalism at the cross-section level with an event-level implementation in which interactions are classified as transparent or non-transparent within the NuWro intranuclear cascade. This unified framework enables realization of FSI effects across inclusive observables and exclusive final states. We demonstrate the impact of this implementation by comparing predictions to both inclusive electron-scattering data and exclusive MicroBooNE measurements of CCQE-dominated observable, showing that the inclusion of FSI leads to a significant improvement in agreement with the data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a modified treatment of final-state interactions in quasielastic lepton-nucleus scattering within the NuWro Monte Carlo generator's spectra function framework, improving agreement with experimental data by unifying inclusive and exclusive FSI descriptions.
AI.motivation: To address the inconsistency between inclusive cross-section calculations and exclusive hadron-propagation descriptions in existing FSI treatments, ensuring better agreement with experimental data.
AI.method: A unified framework combining a convolution-based formalism for cross-section calculations with an event-level implementation classifying interactions as transparent or non-transparent in the NuWro intranuclear cascade.
AI.result: The implementation significantly improves agreement with inclusive electron-scattering data and exclusive MicroBooNE CCQE-dominated observables when FSI effects are included.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a consistent and improved treatment of FSI effects across both inclusive and exclusive observables in lepton-nucleus scattering experiments.

title: Spin-($0$, $1$, $\frac{1}{2}$) Field Perturbations, Quasinormal Modes, Overtones, Greybody Factors and Strong Cosmic Censorship of Einstein-Skyrme Black Holes
summary: We carry out a multi-spin perturbation-theory study of the four-dimensional Einstein-Skyrme (ES) anti-de Sitter (AdS) black hole (BH), whose lapse $f(r)=1-8πK-2M/r+4πKλ/r^{2}$ inherits two couplings from the hadronic model -- the pion combination $K=F_π^{2}/4$ and the Skyrme coupling $e$ -- with $Kλ=1/e^{2}$ pinned by the theory rather than being a free integration constant. After deriving the Klein-Gordon, Maxwell and Dirac effective potentials on this background, we compute the quasinormal modes (QNMs) with the sixth-order WKB formula and cross-check them against the thirteenth-order Padé-improved expansion and the eikonal limit set by the unstable photon sphere. The first overtone $(n=1)$ of the scalar and electromagnetic channels reveals a mild Konoplya-Zhidenko anomaly: the ratio $|\mathrm{Im}\,ω_{1}|/|\mathrm{Im}\,ω_{0}|$ drifts monotonically from $2.42$ to $2.54$, sitting noticeably below the Schwarzschild value near $3$. The dominant scalar mode is independently reproduced to better than $0.2\%$ by a time-domain Prony fit. Greybody factors for all three spins follow the ordering $T_{\rm EM}<T_{\rm scalar}<T_{\rm Dirac}$. Testing strong cosmic censorship at the Cauchy horizon, we find the Christodoulou parameter $β\lesssim 4\times 10^{-3}$ across the admissible $(K,e)$ window -- more than two orders of magnitude below the threshold $1/2$ -- with the margin protected by the theory itself.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates quasinormal modes (QNMs) of a four-dimensional Einstein-Skyrme anti-de Sitter (AdS) black hole using perturbation theory. By analyzing scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac fields, it identifies an anomaly in the frequency ratio of QNMs and confirms strong cosmic censorship with a negligible Christodoulou parameter.
AI.motivation: To explore the properties of Einstein-Skyrme AdS black holes under perturbations, validate theoretical predictions through multi-method cross-checks, and test the robustness of cosmic censorship hypothesis.
AI.method: Multi-spin perturbation theory was applied to derive effective potentials for Klein-Gordon, Maxwell, and Dirac fields. Quasinormal modes were computed using the sixth-order WKB method, Padé approximation, and eikonal limit analysis. Time-domain Prony fitting verified scalar modes. The Christodoulou parameter was evaluated to assess cosmic censorship.
AI.result: (1) Scalar/electromagnetic QNMs show a Konoplya-Zhidenko anomaly with the ratio |Im ω₁|/|Im ω₀| between 2.42-2.54. (2) Dirac greybody factors are largest, followed by scalar then electromagnetic. (3) Christodoulou parameter β < 4×10⁻³, far below the 1/2 threshold, supporting strong cosmic censorship.
AI.conclusion: The Einstein-Skyrme AdS black hole model consistently exhibits deviations in QNM frequency ratios and strongly upholds cosmic censorship. These results validate the theoretical framework's robustness under extreme conditions and reinforce confidence in general relativity's predictive power in such spacetimes.

title: To understand the radiative processes of pulsars and fast radio bursts with the FAST
comment: A review paper, submitted to Chinese Physics Letters
summary: The radiative mechanism of coherent radio emission has remained an enigma since the discovery of pulsars, even the emergence of fast radio bursts (FRBs), which exhibit similarities to the single-pulse behavior of pulsars and have opened a new view for deciphering the long-standing mystery. Besides tremendous efforts in modelling, advanced facilities matter for solving the problem. The authors review the observational breakthroughs from the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST), which are providing pivotal insights to unravel the underlying physics of pulsars and FRBs. This study offers a novel perspective in the era when pulsars meet FRBs, and further investigations are encouraged to utilize the highly sensitive telescope, the FAST.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews FAST's observational breakthroughs in studying pulsars and FRBs, offering new insights into their coherent radio emission mechanisms and highlighting the telescope's role in advancing our understanding of these sources.
AI.motivation: To address the long-standing mystery of the radiative mechanism for coherent radio emission in pulsars and FRBs by leveraging data from the advanced FAST telescope.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive review of observational data obtained from the FAST telescope, focusing on breakthroughs related to pulsar single-pulse behavior and FRB characteristics. They analyze similarities between pulsars and FRBs to propose a unified framework for understanding their emission mechanisms.
AI.result: FAST observations provide pivotal insights into the underlying physics of pulsars and FRBs, suggesting shared mechanisms in their coherent radio emission. Key breakthroughs include detailed single-pulse structures and FRB properties that align with theoretical models, supporting the idea that these phenomena are closely related.
AI.conclusion: FAST's high sensitivity is crucial for future investigations into pulsar and FRB emission mechanisms. The study emphasizes the need for continued observational efforts and interdisciplinary research to fully unravel the enigmatic radiative processes.

title: Induced Multi-phase Inflation with Reheating: Leptogenesis and Dark Matter Production in Metric versus Palatini
summary: We study non-minimally coupled scalar-induced multi-phase inflation in metric and Palatini gravity, considering linear, Brans-Dicke-like, and Higgs-like sectors. The scalar spectral index lies in the range \( n_s \simeq 0.93 \ \text{--} \ 0.98 \), consistent with \textit{Planck} and combined \textit{Planck}+ACT data. The tensor-to-scalar ratio can reach \( r \sim 0.03 \) in metric, whereas Palatini models generically predict \( r \lesssim 10^{-5} \). In the Palatini case, field excursions remain sub-Planckian, and the perturbative unitarity cutoff is raised. Reheating proceeds via perturbative inflaton decays into Higgs bosons and fermionic dark matter (DM) through the portal coupling \( λ_{12} \) and Yukawa coupling \( y_χ\). Radiative stability of the inflationary plateau constrains the couplings to \( y_χ, λ_{12} \sim 10^{-7} \ \text{--} \ 10^{-3} \), implying \( 4\,\mathrm{MeV} \lesssim T_{\rm rh} \lesssim 10^{15}\,\mathrm{GeV} \). Palatini realizations require smaller couplings and thus a narrower reheating window. Non-thermal DM production $χ$ from inflaton decays is viable for DM mass \( m_χ\sim \mathrm{keV} \ \text{--} \ \mathrm{PeV} \) with \( y_χ\lesssim 10^{-6} \) over large parameter regions. We estimate the inflaton-right-handed neutrino (RHN) Yukawa coupling \( y_N \) required for successful baryogenesis via non-thermal leptogenesis within a Type-I seesaw framework, for the lightest RHN mass \( M_{N_1} \sim 10^{9} \ \text{--} \ 10^{14}\,\mathrm{GeV} \), provided \( M_{N_1} > T_{\rm max} \), where \( T_{\rm max} \) follows from radiatively consistent reheating. In Palatini scenarios, the lower maximal temperature and tighter stability bounds further restrict the leptogenesis parameter space.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates scalar-induced multi-phase inflation in both metric and Palatani gravity models, focusing on non-minimal coupling effects. Key findings include the scalar spectral index within observational limits, significantly lower tensor-to-scalar ratios in Palatini models, and constraints on couplings and reheating temperatures. It also explores viable non-thermal dark matter production and non-thermal leptogenesis for baryogenesis under specific coupling and mass conditions.
AI.motivation: To explore how non-minimally coupled scalar fields in multi-phase inflation scenarios can reconcile theoretical predictions with Planck and ACT observational data, particularly addressing reheating mechanisms, dark matter production, and leptogenesis feasibility in different gravitational frameworks.
AI.method: The study examines linear, Brans-Dicke-like, and Higgs-like sectors within metric and Palatini gravity. It calculates scalar spectral indices, tensor-to-scalar ratios, and analyzes reheating via perturbative decays, radiative stability constraints, and non-thermal production of dark matter and right-handed neutrinos for leptogenesis.
AI.result: Palatini models yield extremely low tensor-to-scalar ratios (r ~1e-5) with sub-Planckian field excursions, while metric models reach r~0.03. Reheating temperatures constrain couplings (ychi, λ12 ~1e-7 to 1e-3), enabling keV-PeV dark matter via non-thermal production. Leptogenesis via non-thermal RHNs is viable for specific mass ranges (1e9-1e14 GeV) if MN1 > T_max, though Palatini scenarios restrict this further due to lower temperatures.
AI.conclusion: Multi-phase inflation in Palatini gravity offers a viable framework with observational consistency and constrained parameter spaces for dark matter and leptogenesis, but stricter conditions on couplings and reheating temperatures. Metric models allow broader parameter freedom but face tension with high tensor modes. Results highlight the importance of gravitational framework choice in cosmological model building.

title: Homothetic Killing horizons in generic Vaidya spacetimes
summary: We study the conformal Killing equation for generic Vaidya-like spacetimes, including those with rotation. We show that these spacetimes admit a unique class of conformal Killing vectors that are homothetic for mass, charge, or rotation parameters being linear functions of the advanced null-time. For the Kerr-Vaidya metric, the solution to the conformal Killing equation exists iff both mass and rotation parameters become dynamic. The presence of a homothetic Killing vector (HKV) for such a spacetime enables one to conformally map the original dynamical spacetime to a stationary spacetime, enabling access to the standard methods pertaining to a Killing horizon. The surface where an HKV becomes null is termed the homothetic Killing horizon. We discuss the thermodynamic properties of such homothetic Killing horizons and formulate a version of the first law (or flux balance law) for spherically symmetric Vaidya spacetimes. We further study the maximal analytic extension of a charged Vaidya metric and indicate its implications for studying particle creation in such backgrounds.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates conformal Killing vectors in Vaidya-like spacetimes, including those with rotation, and finds a unique class of homothetic Killing vectors under specific parameter conditions. It explores thermodynamic properties and formulates a first law for spherical Vaidya spacetimes, while also examining analytic extensions and particle creation implications for charged Vaidya metrics.
AI.motivation: To understand the symmetry properties of dynamic spacetimes (Vaidya-like) with varying mass, charge, or rotation parameters, and to extend thermodynamic analysis and methods applicable to static spacetimes to these dynamic cases.
AI.method: Solving the conformal Killing equation for general Vaidya and Kerr-Vaidya metrics, identifying conditions for homothetic Killing vectors existence (e.g., dynamic mass and rotation), conformally mapping to stationary spacetimes, deriving thermodynamic laws, and performing maximal extension analysis.
AI.result: Identified a unique homothetic Killing vector class for dynamic parameters, established existence conditions for Kerr-Vaidya, formulated a first-law type flux balance for spherical cases, and analyzed the charged Vaidya's maximal extension linking it to particle creation studies.
AI.conclusion: Homothetic Killing vectors provide a tool to treat dynamic spacetimes using static spacetime techniques, enabling thermodynamic analysis and horizon studies. The results offer new insights into dynamical black hole evolutions and semiclassical effects in such backgrounds.

title: Learning What's Real: Disentangling Signal and Measurement Artifacts in Multi-Sensor Data, with Applications to Astrophysics
comment: Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Foundation Models for Science at ICLR 2026. 10 pages, 6 figures, plus appendix
summary: Data collected from the physical world is always a combination of multiple sources: an underlying signal from the physical process of interest and a signal from measurement-dependent artifacts from the sensor or instrument. This secondary signal acts as a confounding factor, limiting our ability to extract information about the physics underlying the phenomena we observe. Furthermore, it complicates the combination of observations in heterogeneous or multi-instrument settings. We propose a deep learning framework that leverages overlapping observations, a dual-encoder architecture, and a counterfactual generation objective to disentangle these factors of variation. The resulting representations explicitly separate intrinsic signals from sensor-specific distortions and noise, and can be used for counterfactual view generation, parameter inference unconfounded by measurement distortions, and instrument-independent similarity search. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on astrophysical galaxy images from the DESI Legacy Imaging Survey (Legacy) and the Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC) Survey as a representative multi-instrument setting. This framework provides a general recipe for scientific and multi-modal self-supervised pretraining: construct training pairs from overlapping observations of the same physical system, treat sensor- or modality-specific effects as augmentations, and learn invariant representations through counterfactual generation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a deep learning framework that uses a dual-encoder architecture and counterfactual generation to separate intrinsic physical signals from sensor-specific artifacts in multi-instrument data, enabling applications like parameter inference and instrument-independent analysis.
AI.motivation: To address confounding factors from sensor-dependent artifacts in multi-instrument settings, which limit information extraction about physical processes and hinder data integration.
AI.method: A dual-encoder deep learning framework with a counterfactual generation objective, trained on overlapping observations to disentangle intrinsic signals and sensor-specific distortions. Treats sensor effects as augmentations and learns invariant representations.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated on astrophysical galaxy images from DESI Legacy and HSC surveys, enabling counterfactual view generation, unbiased parameter inference, and instrument-independent similarity search.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a generalizable approach for scientific and multi-modal self-supervised learning, offering solutions to separate physics from sensor effects across instruments.

title: Impact of Observational and Modelling Assumptions on Intergalactic Magnetic Field Constraints from TeV Gamma-Ray Bursts with the Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory
comment: Contribution to the 2026 Very High Energy Phenomena in the Universe session of the 60th Rencontres de Moriond
summary: The Intergalactic Magnetic Field (IGMF), permeating cosmic voids, is thought to be a relic of primordial magnetic fields generated in the early Universe and that gave rise to all astrophysical magnetic fields. While it has escaped direct detection, lower limits on its intensity can be derived by characterising the time-delayed secondary emission initiated when primary very high-energy (VHE) photons from gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) produce lepton pairs that are deflected by the IGMF before generating a secondary gamma-ray flux. Most current studies exclude IGMF values below $10^{-18}\;\mathrm{G}$, however, they are typically performed under idealised conditions. Focusing on the impact of modelling and observational choices, we simulate CTAO observations of GRBs 190114C and 221009A under varying conditions. For GRB 190114C-like sources, we establish a stable lower limit of $2\times10^{-16}\;\mathrm{G}$, robust against most variations in source properties and detection strategies. For more extreme GRB 221009A-like events, we demonstrate that CTAO could probe fields up to at least $10^{-16}\;\mathrm{G}$ under harsh conditions, improving significantly the current IGMF constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates lower limits of the intergalactic magnetic field (IGMF) by simulating Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory (CTAO) observations of two gamma-ray bursts (GRBs). They find improved lower limits of 2×10^−16 G for GRB 190114C-like sources and up to 10^−16 G for GRB 221009A-like events under harsh conditions, surpassing previous constraints.
AI.motivation: To refine estimates of the IGMF's intensity by addressing limitations of prior studies that relied on idealized assumptions, using more realistic simulations of GRB observations with CTAO.
AI.method: Simulating CTAO observations of GRBs 190114C and 221009A under varying conditions to model secondary gamma-ray emission resulting from primary VHE photons interacting with the IGMF. The deflection of lepton pairs by the IGMF was analyzed to derive conservative lower limits on its strength.
AI.result: For GRB 190114C-like events, a lower limit of 2×10^−16 G was consistently found. For GRB 221009A-like events, CTAO could probe fields up to 10^−16 G even under challenging scenarios, significantly enhancing current IGMF constraints.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating realistic observational and modeling choices, this study establishes stronger lower bounds for the IGMF than previous work, highlighting the potential of CTAO for future IGMF studies and the importance ofConsidering diverse GRB characteristics in refining cosmic magnetic field constraints.

title: Toward selective quantum advantage in hadronic tomography:explicit cases from Compton form factors, GPDs, TMDs, and GTMDs
summary: We recast the case for quantum advantage in hadronic physics as an observable-by-observable question rather than a blanket claim about Quantum Chromo-Dynamics (QCD). Focusing on hadronic tomography, we analyze why Compton form factors (CFF), generalized parton distributions (GPDs), Transverse Momentum-dependent Distributions (TMDs), and Generalized Transverse Momentum-dependent Distributions (GTMDs) are natural quantum targets: they are defined by light-front, off-forward, or real-time correlation functions whose extraction from Euclidean calculations or sparse experimental data is often an ill-posed inverse problem. We separate three notions of advantage -- algorithmic, computational, and representational -- and connect each to explicit formal objects. At the algorithmic level, Hamiltonian simulation, linear-response algorithms, and amplitude-estimation primitives motivate gains for real-time and sign-problematic observables. At the computational level, direct quantum evaluation of matrix elements and correlators becomes plausible for PDFs, GPDs, timelike response, and high-energy evolution. At the inference level, recent Quantum Deep Neural Network (QDNN) studies of CFF extraction indicate improved performance in noisy and sparse regimes and motivate hybrid fits in which a quantum simulator supplies a physics prior while a classical network models detector and nuisance effects. We discuss why real-device execution is scientifically necessary, summarize current hardware milestones, and propose benchmark criteria for credible claims of quantum advantage in hadronic tomography.

AI.tldr: The paper redefines the case for quantum advantage in hadronic physics by examining individual observables like Compton form factors (CFF), GPDs, TMDs, and GTMDs. It identifies three types of quantum advantages—algorithmic, computational, and representational—linking each to specific theoretical tools and computational methods. The authors argue that quantum computing can address the challenges of extracting these observables from Euclidean calculations or sparse experimental data, proposing hybrid quantum-classical approaches for improved inference.
AI.motivation: To evaluate quantum advantage specifically for hadronic tomography observables (CFF, GPDs, TMDs, GTMDs), addressing the ill-posed inverse problems inherent in their extraction from Euclidean QCD calculations or limited experimental data.
AI.method: The paper categorizes advantages into three types—algorithmic (Hamiltonian simulation, linear-response algorithms, amplitude estimation), computational (direct quantum evaluation of matrix elements), and representational (QDNNs for noisy data inference). It connects these to relevant formal objects and proposes hybrid quantum-classical inference models.
AI.result: Identifies specific applications where quantum methods outperform classical approaches, such as handling real-time dynamics, sign problems, and sparse data. Highlights QDNNs' improved performance in CFF extraction and sets benchmark criteria for quantum advantage claims.
AI.conclusion: Quantum computing offers distinct advantages in hadronic physics through algorithmic, computational, and representational improvements. Real-device execution and hybrid methods are essential for credible advancements, with proposed benchmarks to assess success.

title: Testing the Kerr hypothesis beyond the quadrupole with GW241011
comment: 1 figure in the main text, 2 figures in the Supplementary material
summary: All multipole moments of a Kerr black hole are uniquely determined by its mass and spin. Gravitational wave observations can test this prediction by measuring spin-induced multipole moments imprinted on the inspiral phase of compact binary mergers. In this Letter, we show that the recently reported compact binary coalescence GW241011 enables a simultaneous test of deviations in the spin-induced quadrupole and octupole moments of the binary components from their black hole values. We find no evidence for deviations from the Kerr prediction and place the first constraints on spin-induced octupole moments of the compact binary. This approach complements tests of the Kerr nature of compact binary merger remnants based on quasinormal mode measurements in the ringdown phase.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses testing the Kerr black hole's multipole moment predictions using gravitational wave observations from GW241011, finding no deviations and setting constraints on octupole moments.
AI.motivation: To test the uniqueness of Kerr black hole multipole moments and verify if gravitational wave data supports the mass and spin as sole determinants of these moments.
AI.method: Analyzing the inspiral phase of the compact binary coalescence GW241011 to measure spin-induced quadrupole and octupole moments, comparing them against Kerr predictions.
AI.result: No significant deviations from Kerr's predictions were found, and the first constraints on spin-induced octupole moments in compact binaries were established.
AI.conclusion: The results support the Kerr black hole model and highlight the effectiveness of using inspiral phase data alongside ringdown quasinormal modes for testing black hole properties.

title: PEACC -- Precision Emitter for 21 cm Array Coherent Calibration
summary: Foreground mitigation remains a central challenge for 21 cm intensity mapping experiments, which require precise, wideband calibration of telescope beams and gains. We present the Precision Emitter for 21 cm Array Coherent Calibration (PEACC), a digitally synthesized calibration source that generates Gaussian noise across a 1.2 GHz bandwidth, time-synchronized to a 1 pulse-per-second output from a GPS-disciplined oscillator, and optimized for aerial deployment. PEACC uses a dual-source architecture with one unit mounted on an aerial platform and a second reference unit connected directly to the radio data acquisition system; this configuration enables improved sensitivity in the low-SNR regime and direct phase measurement. The system further supports configurable band selection, allowing adaptation to various 21 cm intensity mapping telescopes. We validated PEACC through anechoic chamber measurements and by integrating the source on a drone flown over a local radio dish testbed. In both settings, the correlated channel substantially outperformed the auto-correlation channel across all signal-to-noise regimes of interest, confirming the key advantage of the dual-source architecture. To our knowledge, this is the first published demonstration of a free-space coherent calibration signal synchronized only by clocks, the first deployment of such a source on a drone, and the first published beam measurements made with such a source. Given the growing interest in drone-based calibration for 21 cm arrays, this work establishes the feasibility of high-fidelity digital calibration for next-generation 21 cm instruments, and provides a practical path towards improved foreground control and beam calibration in future arrays.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PEACC, a calibration system for 21 cm intensity mapping experiments. It uses a dual-source setup with one unit on an aerial drone and another on the ground, enabling precise calibration via coherent signals synchronized by GPS clocks. Validation shows superior performance over traditional methods, offering a new approach for future radio telescope arrays.
AI.motivation: Foreground mitigation and precise calibration are critical for 21 cm experiments. Existing methods struggle with wideband calibration and low-SNR conditions, necessitating a reliable, deployable solution.
AI.method: PEACC generates synchronized Gaussian noise across 1.2 GHz, using a dual-source architecture: an aerial unit on a drone and a ground reference. It enables phase measurement and configurable band selection, validated via anechoic chamber tests and drone-flight integration with a radio dish.
AI.result: PEACC's correlated channel outperformed auto-correlation in all tested SNR regimes. It achieved first-of-its-kind demonstrations: clock-synchronized free-space calibration, drone-deployed source, and beam measurements using this method.
AI.conclusion: PEACC establishes drone-based calibration as feasible for next-gen 21 cm instruments, improving foreground control and beam calibration. It offers a practical pathway for enhancing radio telescope calibration accuracy and scalability.

title: Joint Observation of SGR J1935+2154 with \textit{Insight}-HXMT and KM40m during the active episode of October 2022
summary: SGR J1935+2154 is the unique magnetar so far from which fast radio bursts have been detected. In October 2022, it resumed its burst activity, and we implemented a dedicated target-of-opportunity (ToO) observation on it from Oct. 13th to Nov. 1st, 2022 (about 940 ks in total) with \textit{Insight}-HXMT, while the KM40m radio telescope observed this source for about 1400 hours since Oct. 15th. We searched the LE, ME, and HE data of \textit{Insight}-HXMT in the overlapping observation time windows with the KM40m radio telescope and revealed 60 magnetar X-ray bursts (MXBs), while KM40m only detected 1 radio burst. In particular, we find that there is an X-ray burst on October 21 (denoted as MXB 221021) temporally associated with this radio burst. Interestingly, this association event shows very different morphology from those X-ray and radio association events from this source reported before (e.g., MXB/FRB 200428). Moreover, we systematically analyzed the temporal and spectral properties of the sample of MXBs during this observation and found that % the (radio-associated) MXB 221021 shows some different properties from other MXBs without associated radio bursts. These findings shed new light on the physical mechanisms of X-ray bursts and radio burst emission in magnetars.

AI.tldr: The study investigates X-ray bursts (MXBs) from SGR J1935+2154 during its 2022 activity using Insight-HXMT and KM40m data, finding 60 MXBs with only 1 radio burst detected. A specific X-ray burst (MXB 221021) coincided with the radio burst but showed distinct properties compared to previous associations, offering new insights into magnetar emission mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To understand the physical mechanisms linking X-ray and radio burst emissions in magnetars, particularly following the unique association of SGR J1935+2154 as the only magnetar known to produce fast radio bursts (FRBs).
AI.method: The team conducted a dedicated 940 ks Insight-HXMT observation (covering X-ray bands LE, ME, HE) and paired it with KM40m radio telescope data (1400 hours) to correlate X-ray and radio burst activity. They analyzed 60 MXBs, focusing on the October 21 event linked to a radio burst, comparing its temporal/spectral traits with non-associated MXBs.
AI.result: 60 MXBs were detected, with only 1 radio burst observed. The associated MXB 221021 exhibited dissimilar morphology and properties from prior X-ray/radio burst pairs (e.g., contrasting with the 200428 event). This discrepancy suggests varying emission mechanisms or environmental conditions.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that the relationship between X-ray and radio bursts in magnetars is more complex than previously thought, with MXB 221021's unique characteristics suggesting different physical processes at play compared to earlier associations. This demands re-evaluation of theoretical models for magnetar emissions.

title: Chiral Condensation and Chiral Phase Diagram under Combined Rotation and Chemical Potential in Holographic QCD
summary: We investigate the combined effects of rotation and finite quark chemical potential on inhomogeneous chiral condensation and the chiral phase diagram within the soft-wall holographic QCD model. Using the five-dimensional AdS-RN metric, we study the spatial profile of the chiral condensate and the resulting $T - Ω$ phase diagram under Neumann and Dirichlet boundary conditions. Increasing $Ω$ induces strong spatial inhomogeneity: the condensate is suppressed more strongly near the edge than at the center, and vanishes at the boundary when $Ω$ exceeds a critical value. The chemical potential $μ$ acts as a global suppression factor, reducing the condensate magnitude without altering the spatial pattern. The $T - Ω$ phase diagrams are investigated for different chemical potentials .For the case $μ$ = 0, they are also studied at different distances from the rotation axis. It is found that both $Ω$ and $μ$ lower the chiral phase transition temperature, and their suppression effects are additive. In a rotating system, the critical temperature becomes position-dependent, decreasing with increasing distance from the rotation axis. These findings reveal a rich, spatially dependent phase structure in rotating QCD matter, relevant for non-central heavy-ion collisions.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how rotation and quark chemical potential affect inhomogeneous chiral condensation and the phase diagram using a holographic QCD model.
AI.motivation: To understand the combined effects of rotation and finite quark chemical potential on chiral symmetry breaking and phase transitions in QCD matter, relevant to non-central heavy-ion collisions.
AI.method: Employed the soft-wall holographic QCD model with AdS-RN metric to analyze spatial chiral condensate profiles and phase diagrams under Neumann and Dirichlet conditions.
AI.result: Rotation (Ω) induces spatial inhomogeneity in the condensate, suppressing it more at edges. Chemical potential (μ) globally reduces condensate magnitude. Both Ω and μ lower the chiral phase transition temperature additively. Critical temperature decreases with distance from the rotation axis.
AI.conclusion: Rotating QCD matter exhibits a complex spatially dependent chiral phase structure, important for interpreting non-central heavy-ion collision experiments.

title: Birkhoff rigidity from a covariant optical seed
summary: We present a local seed-to--Kerr--Schild route to Birkhoff rigidity in four-dimensional spherical vacuum gravity. On the two-dimensional orbit space, the areal radius \(r\) determines a scalar \(F:=-(\nabla r)^2\), and the reduced vacuum equations imply \(F(r)=1-2M/r\). We show that the normalized one-forms \(dr/F\) and \((*dr)/F\) are closed, so that the null combinations \(F^{-1}(dr\pm *dr)\) are exact null seed forms. Integrating these yields local Eddington--Finkelstein coordinates in which the metric takes Kerr--Schild form over a flat background. We then prove the corresponding uniqueness statement in the stationary optical sector: spherical symmetry forces the inverse optical seed \(\mathcal R\) to equal \(\pm r\), equivalently the optical seed \(ρ\) to equal \(\mp 1/r\), and the resulting seed data reconstruct the Schwarzschild family. Thus, Birkhoff rigidity is paired with a spherical converse theorem in the stationary optical framework: Schwarzschild is the unique spherically symmetric stationary vacuum Kerr--Schild geometry generated by a nowhere-vanishing optical seed.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a local seed-to-Kerr-Schild route to Birkhoff rigidity in four-dimensional spherical vacuum gravity, demonstrating that the Schwarzschild solution is the unique spherically symmetric stationary vacuum Kerr-Schild geometry with a nowhere-vanishing optical seed.
AI.motivation: To establish a new method for proving Birkhoff's theorem (uniqueness of Schwarzschild solution under spherical symmetry) using optical geometry and seed forms in the Kerr-Schild framework.
AI.method: Uses areal radius r to derive scalar F=1-2M/r, shows one-forms dr/F and *dr/F are closed, integrates to Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates, proves uniqueness via spherical converse theorem in the stationary optical sector.
AI.result: Derives exact null seed forms leading to Kerr-Schild metric over flat background, proves Schwarzschild is unique solution under stated conditions, links Birkhoff's theorem to seed data uniqueness.
AI.conclusion: Birkhoff rigidity is validated through optical seed analysis, showing Schwarzschild's uniqueness arises from spherical symmetry constraints on seed data in the Kerr-Schild formulation.

title: A Multi-modal Fusion Network for Star-Galaxy Classification from CSST Simulated Datasets
summary: The distinction between stars and galaxies is a fundamental problem in the field of celestial classification. This issue has become challenging for these ongoing and upcoming digital surveys, which will produce terabytes and even petabytes of astronomical data. While deep learning offers a powerful solution for star-galaxy classification in large-scale datasets, most current approaches are limited by their reliance on catalog data alone, which consists primarily of multi-band magnitudes and imprecise morphological parameters. Therefore, we utilize China Space Station Telescope (CSST) simulation data to build a dataset with both image and photometric catalog, including 32,371 stars and 93,525 galaxies. A supervised deep learning network based on ResNet-50 and BiLSTM is proposed to improve the classification of two types of astronomical objects. The features of the catalog and image are integrated by the model, achieving 99.81% recall for galaxies and 99.66% recall for stars after training on GPU for 50 epochs. We evaluated the effects of data augmentation and multi-modal data fusion, which demonstrate that our model has commendable performance. Furthermore, our model also has a high accuracy rate for faint astronomical objects and high redshift galaxies, demonstrating its applicability to the upcoming CSST scientific data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a deep learning model combining ResNet-50 and BiLSTM to classify stars and galaxies using both image and catalog data from CSST simulations, achieving high recall rates and effectiveness for faint objects.
AI.motivation: Current star-galaxy classification methods rely solely on catalog data with limited morphological parameters, making them inadequate for processing large-scale survey data like those from the CSST. The need arises for a model that leverages multi-modal data (images and catalogs) to enhance accuracy and handle increasing data volumes.
AI.method: The model integrates ResNet-50 (for image features) and BiLSTM (for catalog features), fusing their outputs. It uses CSST simulation data comprising 32,371 stars and 93,525 galaxies, with experiments on data augmentation and multi-modal fusion.
AI.result: Achieved 99.81% recall for galaxies and 99.66% recall for stars after 50 epochs. Demonstrated high accuracy for faint objects and high-redshift galaxies, proving suitability for CSST data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model effectively improves star-galaxy classification by combining image and catalog data, offering scalable solutions for upcoming large astronomical surveys.

title: Lorentz invariance violation search with flaring active galactic nuclei observations of the first Large-Sized Telescope of CTAO
comment: contribution to the 2026 Very High Energy Phenomena in the Universe session of the 60th Rencontres de Moriond, 4 pages, 1 figure
summary: The rapid variability observed in very-high-energy (VHE) sources-such as pulsars, gamma-ray bursts (GRBs), and flares from active galactic nuclei (AGN)-can be used to detect or constrain a potential violation of Lorentz invariance (LIV). These effects can be investigated by measuring time lags in the arrival of VHE photons. However, an important source of uncertainty arises from intrinsic processes within the sources themselves that may induce photon delays unrelated to LIV. To address this challenge, we aim to combine observations of different sources, located at different redshifts. In this study, we present the results of a standardized analysis applied to all AGN observations conducted by the first Large-Sized Telescope of the upcoming Cherenkov Telescope Array Observatory. Our analysis includes a systematic search for intra-night variability in archival data from nights with significant excess detections for target candidates. By combining these observations, we derive constraints on the characteristic energy scales at which LIV deterministic or stochastic effects are expected to manifest.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes rapid variability in VHE sources like AGN using Cherenkov Telescope data to distinguish LIV-induced photon delays from intrinsic source effects, setting constraints on energy scales for LIV.
AI.motivation: To detect or constrain Lorentz Invariance Violation (LIV) effects by minimizing uncertainties from intrinsic photon delays in sources.
AI.method: Standardized analysis of AGN observations from the first Large-Sized Telescope, systematic search for intra-night variability in archival data with significant excess detections.
AI.result: Derivation of constraints on energy scales where deterministic or stochastic LIV effects might occur, using combined observations at different redshifts.
AI.conclusion: Combining multi-source observations at varying redshifts effectively reduces intrinsic variability uncertainties, improving LIV constraint accuracy.

title: William A. Bardeen -- A Brief Biography
summary: William Allan Bardeen (September 15, 1941 $-$ November 18, 2025) was an American theoretical physicist who worked at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory. He is renowned for his foundational work on the chiral anomaly, the Adler-Bardeen theorem, the non-Abelian anomaly and gravitational anomalies. He was instrumental in the development of quantum chromodynamics and its applications, such as semileptonic decays and the $Λ_{\overline{MS}}$ scheme frequently used in perturbative analysis of high energy processes involving strong interactions. Bardeen also played a major role in developing a theory of dynamical breaking of electroweak symmetry via top quark condensates, leading to one of the first composite Brout-Englert-Higgs boson models. His work on the chiral symmetry dynamics of heavy-light quark bound states correctly predicted abnormally long-lived resonances which are chiral symmetry partners of the ground state.

AI.tldr: The obituary highlights William Allan Bardeen's contributions to theoretical physics, particularly his work on chiral anomaly, Adler-Bardeen theorem, non-Abelian anomalies, gravitational anomalies, quantum chromodynamics (QCD), and composite Brout-Englert-Higgs boson models based on top quark condensation.
AI.motivation: To recognize Bardeen's significant contributions to theoretical physics and summarize his key achievements in QCD, anomalies, and Higgs mechanism alternatives.
AI.method: The analysis reviews Bardeen's published works, including his research on quantum field theory anomalies, applications of QCD in particle physics processes, and model-building for electroweak symmetry breaking.
AI.result: Bardeen established foundational concepts in anomaly physics and QCD applications, contributed to Higgs boson model development, and made accurate predictions about chiral symmetry-related particle lifetimes.
AI.conclusion: Bardeen's legacy lies in advancing quantum field theory through anomaly studies, shaping modern QCD frameworks, and pioneering composite Higgs boson theories that remain influential in particle physics.

title: Relativistic figures of equilibrium in the Wald magnetosphere
summary: We consider a self-gravitating, rigidly rotating charged perfect fluid immersed in the Wald magnetosphere, constructed out of two linearly independent Killing vectors present in stationary and axially-symmetric spacetimes. We show that in non-vacuum spacetimes, Wald's solution can be compatible with the electric current associated with a rotating charged perfect fluid characterized by the vanishing electric conductivity. We prove that for rigidly rotating fluids with a constant energy density or described by the polytropic equation of state, the resulting equations expressing the conservation of the energy-momentum tensor can be integrated. Consequently, the system can be described by nearly standard Einstein-Euler equations known from the theory of general-relativistic rotating fluids, with modifications introduced in the Euler-Bernoulli equation. Numerical solutions of the Einstein-Euler equations are provided for these two cases by introducing suitable modifications in the pseudospectral code by Ansorg, Kleinwächter, and Meinel.

AI.tldr: The paper examines a self-gravitating, rigidly rotating charged perfect fluid in a Wald magnetosphere using Killing vectors, demonstrating compatibility with vanishing electric conductivity and deriving integrable equations for specific fluid cases, solved numerically using modified Einstein-Euler equations.
AI.motivation: To explore the compatibility of Wald's magnetosphere with charged fluids and derive solvable equations for relativistic rotating fluids under specific conditions.
AI.method: Employing Killing vectors in stationary, axially-symmetric spacetimes to model the fluid, proving integrability for constant energy density or polytropic fluids, and solving Einstein-Euler equations numerically with a modified pseudospectral code.
AI.result: Shows that conservation equations are integrable for specified fluid cases, with numerical solutions obtained using adapted computational methods.
AI.conclusion: Wald's solution supports charged rotating fluids with zero conductivity, enabling general-relativistic fluid dynamics analysis via modified Euler equations.

title: A seeing measurement device for the PoET solar telescope
comment: Accepted for publication in RAS Techniques and Instruments
summary: Atmospheric seeing arises from stochastic fluctuations in the refractive index of the Earth's atmosphere, producing random variations in the apparent direction of incoming light from astronomical sources. Scintillation refers to the associated intensity fluctuations induced by these refractive index inhomogeneities. A quantitative relationship between seeing and scintillation was established in 1993, enabling daytime seeing measurements by exploiting the Sun as an extended, bright source and using non-telescopic instrumentation. PoET, the Paranal solar ESPRESSO Telescope, will feed the Echelle SPectrograph for Rocky Exoplanets and Stable Spectroscopic Observations, ESPRESSO, at the European Southern Observatory (ESO) Very Large Telescope (VLT). By using the Sun as a proxy for solar-type stars, PoET will facilitate detailed investigations of the physical processes that drive stellar noise in ultra-high-precision radial-velocity measurements for exoplanet studies. The instrument is capable of targeting any region on the solar disk and acquiring spatially resolved spectra over areas ranging from 1 to 55 arcseconds. Accurate characterization of daytime atmospheric seeing is therefore essential for selecting the optimal observing aperture and ensuring the scientific performance of PoET. To support this requirement, we have developed and implemented a dedicated solar seeing monitor for daytime deployment at Paranal, Chile, where PoET will operate. In this work, we describe the instrument design and present the results from commissioning and initial on-sky validation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a solar seeing monitor for the PoET telescope to characterize daytime atmospheric seeing, crucial for optimizing exoplanet studies using the Sun as a proxy.
AI.motivation: Accurate daytime seeing measurements are essential for selecting the optimal observing aperture and ensuring the scientific performance of PoET in studying stellar noise for exoplanet research.
AI.method: The developed solar seeing monitor is deployed at Paranal, Chile. It uses non-telescopic instrumentation to exploit the Sun as an extended bright source, leveraging a known quantitative relationship between seeing and scintillation established in 1993. The instrument captures spatially resolved spectra over various apertures to analyze atmospheric effects.
AI.result: Commissioning results and initial validation demonstrate the monitor's effectiveness in characterizing seeing conditions, enabling PoET to achieve stable spectroscopic observations required for ultra-high-precision radial-velocity measurements.
AI.conclusion: The solar seeing monitor successfully supports PoET's objective to investigate stellar noise mechanisms by providing critical atmospheric data, enhancing the reliability of exoplanet detection through radial-velocity methods.

title: Multi-TeV $γ$-ray candidates from GRB 221009A: a downturn in the intrinsic $γ$-ray spectrum, an echo of the prompt emission phase, and intergalactic electromagnetic cascades
summary: The detection of $γ$-ray candidates up to the energy of $\approx$13 TeV from the exceptionally bright $γ$-ray burst GRB 221009A by the Large High Altitude Air-Shower Observatory (LHAASO) has raised considerable interest in the astrophysical community. The $γ$-ray dataset resulting from the LHAASO observations allows one to reconstruct the intrinsic spectrum of GRB 221009A with an unprecedented precision. This intrinsic spectrum reveals a downturn at the energy of several TeV (statistical significance $\gt 5 σ$), i.e. the reconstructed intensity is below the intensity expected for a power-law spectrum. We show that a significant TeV $γ$-ray component may be produced by neutrons from photohadronic interactions inside the fireball. These neutrons escape the fireball and interact with the surrounding matter, giving rise to a flux of electrons and positrons, eventually resulting in an observable flux of GeV--TeV synchrotron photons -- a high energy "echo" of the GRB prompt emission phase. Finally, we show that at multi-TeV energies the contribution of $γ$ rays from intergalactic electromagnetic cascades initiated by primary ultra high energy protons is severely limited during the early afterglow phase for the typical magnetic field strength in the intergalactic filaments above 1 nG.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes γ-ray emissions from GRB 221009A observed by LHAASO, identifying a downturn in the intrinsic spectrum above several TeV. It proposes that this is due to neutron interactions within the fireball, leading to a GeV-TeV photon 'echo.' Additionally, it limits the contribution of UHE protons to multi-TeV γ-rays during early afterglow phases under intergalactic magnetic fields ≥1 nG.
AI.motivation: To explain the unexpected downturn in the γ-ray spectrum of GRB 221009A at TeV energies and evaluate the role of neutron interactions and intergalactic cascades in shaping its emission.
AI.method: Analysis of LHAASO's high-energy γ-ray data to reconstruct the GRB's intrinsic spectrum. Simulated neutron escape and interactions in the fireball environment, followed by synchrotron emission modeling. Calculated constraints on UHE proton-induced cascades under intergalactic magnetic field assumptions.
AI.result: Observed TeV downturn confirmed (≥5σ), attributed to neutron production and subsequent synchrotron photon emission. Found that UHE proton cascades contribute negligibly to multi-TeγV rays under typical intergalactic magnetic field strengths.
AI.conclusion: Neutron-mediated processes and intergalactic photon cascades jointly explain the observed γ-ray spectrum features of GRB 221009A, offering new insights into high-energy astrophysical processes in GRB fireballs.

title: DREAMuS: Dark matter REsearch with Advanced Muon Source
summary: We propose DREAMuS, a fixed-target experiment at the High Intensity Heavy-Ion Accelerator Facility (HIAF), to search for muon-philic dark matter mediated by light flavor-violating bosons. DREAMuS is designed to probe the parameter space of a muon-philic dark matter (DM) mediated by a light flavor-violating boson, specifically a vector $Z'$ (or a scalar $φ$) which is produced in muon-nucleus interactions and decays into dark matter particles with a distinctive detector signature. Precision tracking and time-of-flight measurements are used to suppress the Standard Model backgrounds. We find that DREAMuS can achieve competitive sensitivity in the GeV-scale muon-philic dark matter parameter space, reaching sensitivity to couplings at the $10^{-4}$, especially in the few-hundred-MeV region.In addition to a $μ^-$ run, we highlight the potential of a complementary $μ^+$ beam option, further improving sensitivity to dark matter below 200 $\mathrm{MeV}$ by an order of magnitude.

AI.tldr: DREAMuS is a proposed experiment at HIAF to search for muon-philic dark matter via light flavor-violating bosons (Z' or φ), combining precision tracking and timing to suppress backgrounds, offering competitive sensitivity to low-mass DM below 200 MeV.
AI.motivation: To explore GeV-scale dark matter interactions mediated by light flavor-violating bosons, which current experiments have poorly constrained.
AI.method: Utilize muon-nucleus interactions to produce Z' or φ bosons, which decay into dark matter particles. Use precision tracking and time-of-flight to distinguish signal from SM backgrounds. Include both μ⁻ and μ⁺ beam options for enhanced sensitivity below 200 MeV.
AI.result: Proposed sensitivity to couplings at 1e-4 for ~ few-hundred-MeV DM, with μ⁺ option improving sub-200 MeV sensitivity by an order of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: DREAMuS provides a compelling path to probe unexplored dark matter parameter spaces, especially in low-mass regions, leveraging muon beams at HIAF.

title: Constraining geometrically perturbed strange stars with minimal decoupling from heavy millisecond pulsar observations
summary: We present an exact analytical solution to the Einstein field equations describing the internal structure of strange stars in general relativity. A smooth spacetime deformation is introduced via the sinusoidal perturbation $ g(r)=\sin(Ψr^2) $, which naturally generates anisotropy $Δ=P_t-P_r>0$.
